section.title: David and Jonathan
section.sectionRationale: David has just felled Goliath, and two very different responses form at once. The king’s own son, Jonathan, strips off his robe and armor to give them to David and binds himself to him in covenant love, while the king himself curdles with envy the moment the women sing David’s praise above his own. The chapter tracks both threads together, Saul’s mounting jealousy and repeated attempts to be rid of David by the Philistines’ hand, and the LORD’s unmistakable presence with David through it all, culminating in David’s marriage to Michal. It is one movement: covenant loyalty and royal jealousy rising side by side.
section.thesis: As the LORD’s hand rests visibly on David, the heir to the throne freely yields his own claim in a covenant of love while the reigning king, seeing the same hand, is consumed with a jealousy that will not rest until David is destroyed.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Jonathan’s covenant with David, verses 1 to 4; Saul’s jealousy ignited by the women’s song, verses 5 to 9; the LORD with David while Saul is afraid of him, verses 10 to 16; and David’s marriage to Michal amid Saul’s scheming, verses 17 to 30.

verses.text: After David had finished speaking with Saul, the souls of Jonathan and David were knit together, and Jonathan {{ahav|loved}} him as himself.
And from that day Saul kept David with him and did not let him return to his father’s house.
Then Jonathan made a {{berit|covenant}} with David because he loved him as himself.
And Jonathan removed the robe he was wearing and gave it to David, along with his tunic, his sword, his bow, and his belt.

units.label: Saul’s jealous eye
units.note: what more can he have but the kingdom

verses.text: So David marched out and prospered in everything Saul sent him to do, and Saul set him over the men of war. And this was pleasing in the sight of all the people, and of Saul’s officers as well.

As the troops were returning home after David had killed the Philistine, the women came out of all the cities of Israel to meet King Saul with singing and dancing, with joyful songs, and with tambourines and other instruments.
And as the women danced, they sang out: “Saul has slain his thousands, and David his tens of thousands.”
And Saul was furious and resented this song. “They have ascribed tens of thousands to David,” he said, “but only thousands to me. What more can he have but the kingdom?”
And from that day forward Saul kept a {{qana|jealous}} eye on David.

units.label: The LORD with David, Saul afraid
units.note: the LORD was with David but had departed from Saul

verses.text: The next day a spirit of distress sent from God came upon Saul, and he prophesied inside the house while David played the harp as usual. Now Saul was holding a spear,
and he hurled it, thinking, “I will pin David to the wall.” But David eluded him twice.
So Saul was afraid of David, because the LORD was with David but had departed from Saul.
Therefore Saul sent David away and gave him command of a thousand men. David led the troops out to battle and back,
and he continued to prosper in all his ways, because the LORD was with him.
When Saul saw that David was very successful, he was afraid of him.
But all Israel and Judah loved David, because he was leading them out to battle and back.

Then Saul said to David, “Here is my older daughter Merab. I will give her to you in marriage. Only be valiant for me and fight the LORD’s battles.” But Saul was thinking, “I need not raise my hand against him; let the hand of the Philistines be against him.”
And David said to Saul, “Who am I, and what is my family or my father’s clan in Israel, that I should become the son-in-law of the king?”
So when it was time to give Saul’s daughter Merab to David, she was given in marriage to Adriel of Meholah.
Now Saul’s daughter Michal loved David, and when this was reported to Saul, it pleased him.

“I will give her to David,” Saul thought, “so that she may be a snare to him, and the hand of the Philistines may be against him.” So Saul said to David, “For a second time now you can be my son-in-law.”
Then Saul ordered his servants, “Speak to David privately and tell him, ‘Behold, the king is pleased with you, and all his servants love you. Now therefore, become his son-in-law.’”
But when Saul’s servants relayed these words to David, he replied, “Does it seem trivial in your sight to be the son-in-law of the king? I am a poor man and lightly esteemed.”
And the servants told Saul what David had said.
Saul replied, “Say to David, ‘The king desires no other dowry but a hundred Philistine foreskins as revenge on his enemies.’” But Saul intended to cause David’s death at the hands of the Philistines.
When the servants reported these terms to David, he was pleased to become the king’s son-in-law. Before the wedding day arrived,

David and his men went out and killed two hundred Philistines. He brought their foreskins and presented them as payment in full to become the king’s son-in-law. Then Saul gave his daughter Michal to David in marriage.
When Saul realized that the LORD was with David and that his daughter Michal loved David,
he grew even more afraid of David. So from then on Saul was David’s enemy.
Every time the Philistine commanders came out for battle, David was more successful than all of Saul’s officers, so that his name was highly esteemed.

ahav.gloss: To love, to be bound to another in loyal affection. Jonathan’s love for David, verse 1 and again in verse 3, is not sentiment but covenant loyalty, the same word used of Israel’s love owed to the LORD, Deuteronomy 6:5. The heir to the throne loves the man who will replace him.

berit.gloss: Covenant, a binding, solemn agreement sealed with signs and obligations. Jonathan’s covenant with David, sealed by handing over his robe, armor, sword, bow, and belt, formally surrenders the crown prince’s own claim to the throne in favor of the LORD’s anointed.

qana.gloss: To be jealous, envious, or zealously protective. The same root can describe righteous zeal for the LORD, but in Saul it curdles into a corrosive envy of the very man whose success he cannot deny, verse 9, and it will drive the rest of the book’s conflict.

basic.context: Fresh off David’s victory over Goliath, this chapter follows two threads at once. Jonathan, Saul’s own son and heir, is so bound to David in loyal love that he strips off his royal robe and weapons and gives them to David, sealing a covenant. Meanwhile Saul, hearing the women sing that David has killed his tens of thousands to Saul’s mere thousands, is consumed with jealousy. From that day he watches David with a jealous eye, and it grows into repeated, thinly disguised attempts to have David killed by the Philistines, first through military assignments and then through a bride price of a hundred foreskins. Yet through it all the refrain returns again and again: the LORD was with David. The key questions are why Jonathan would give up his own claim to the throne, and why success that should have brought Saul security instead bred fear and hatred.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative describing what actually happened at Saul’s court; it is not a manual instructing every reader to make identical covenants or to expect kings’ sons to hand over robes. What the narrative teaches by pattern and repetition is that the LORD’s presence and blessing cannot be manufactured or seized by human striving, and that covenant loyalty, pictured in Jonathan, stands in stark contrast to self-protective envy, pictured in Saul.
basic.originalLanguages: Jonathan’s ahav, love, for David in verse 1 is covenant loyalty, not mere feeling, and it leads him to cut a berit, covenant, with David in verse 3, sealed by the transfer of his own royal garments and weapons. Saul, by contrast, from verse 9 keeps a qana, jealous, eye on David, the same word that can describe holy zeal turned instead into a corrosive envy that will not rest.
basic.typology: Jonathan, the rightful heir, willingly strips off his own royal robe and gives it to David, the anointed but not yet enthroned king, a small but vivid picture of one who yields his own claim so that God’s chosen king may be clothed and honored. It faintly anticipates the self-emptying of the true King who did not grasp His own glory but gave Himself for others.
basic.christ: David is repeatedly described as the man the LORD is with, hated and hunted by the reigning power precisely because of that presence, yet prospering in everything and beloved by the ordinary people even as the establishment turns against him. He is anointed but not yet enthroned, a pattern the New Testament shows fulfilled in Jesus, who was with the Father’s favor upon Him, opposed by the rulers of His day, and vindicated not by seizing a throne but by receiving one from the Father in due time.
basic.god: God is the one whose invisible presence is the true engine of the whole chapter; every success of David’s is traced to the LORD being with him, and every fear of Saul’s is traced to the LORD having departed from him. God sovereignly protects His anointed even through a jealous king’s repeated Philistine ploys, showing that human hostility cannot thwart what God has purposed.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Samuel 16:13-14 records the Spirit coming upon David and departing from Saul, the hinge this chapter plays out. Philippians 2:5-8 pictures Christ emptying Himself as Jonathan foreshadows in miniature. Proverbs 27:4 warns that jealousy is more dangerous than wrath, exactly what unfolds in Saul.
basic.oneStory: David the shepherd, already anointed in secret, now begins the long road of being loved by some and hunted by others precisely because the LORD is with him, one more link in the chain that leads from the promise of a king from Judah to the greater Son of David, Jesus, who is beloved of the Father, opposed by rulers, and finally given the everlasting throne.

context.literary: The chapter is built in contrasting panels: covenant love, verses 1 to 4, against a backdrop of communal celebration turned sour, verses 5 to 9, then repeated notices of the LORD’s presence with David bracketing Saul’s fear, verses 10 to 16, and finally an extended scene of Saul’s scheming through marriage negotiations, verses 17 to 30. The refrain the LORD was with him threads through the whole chapter as its interpretive key.
context.historical: A robe, sword, bow, and belt were a prince’s own royal and military equipment; giving them away was a public, costly, symbolic act with real political weight in a court where succession mattered. The victory song of the women, a customary way to celebrate a returning army, was ordinary practice that became the spark for Saul’s jealousy simply because of its wording. The bride price of a hundred Philistine foreskins was both a real military demand and a calculated trap, hoping the Philistines would do what Saul dared not do himself.
context.canonical: This chapter sits at the start of David’s long years of exile and danger under Saul, the historical outworking of Samuel’s anointing in chapter 16 and Saul’s rejection there. Jonathan’s covenant of love anticipates the deeper covenant loyalty, hesed, that will mark David’s own kingship and ultimately God’s covenant faithfulness to the line of David culminating in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show, through the contrast of Jonathan’s covenant love and Saul’s jealousy, that the LORD’s presence rests unmistakably on David and cannot be undone by a rejected king’s scheming, and to trace the beginning of David’s long trial at Saul’s hand.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes historical events at Saul’s court; it does not prescribe that believers must form identical formal covenants or expect the same court intrigue. The enduring truths are the goodness of covenant loyalty over self-protective envy, and the LORD’s sovereign, sustaining presence with His anointed.

debates.question: Why did Jonathan give up his own claim to the throne so readily?

views.label: Recognition of the LORD’s choice
views.case: Jonathan already knew, or came to know, that the LORD had chosen David; his covenant is a faith-filled acceptance of God’s sovereign choice over his own dynastic ambition.

views.label: Personal loyalty and affection
views.case: The text emphasizes that Jonathan loved David as himself; the covenant flows first from deep personal affection and only implicitly from awareness of David’s future kingship.

debates.bearing: Either way, Jonathan models a self-giving loyalty that refuses to grasp at what is not his, in sharp contrast to his father’s grasping fear.

debates.question: Was Saul’s jealousy inevitable given his earlier rejection, or a fresh moral failure?

views.label: The fruit of a heart already turned from God
views.case: Since the Spirit had departed from Saul, chapter 16, his jealousy is the natural fruit of a heart no longer anchored in trust in the LORD.

views.label: A fresh, culpable choice
views.case: Saul is not merely a passive victim of his earlier rejection; each new act, from throwing the spear to plotting the Philistine trap, is a fresh, willful sin for which he remains responsible.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that Saul is genuinely guilty; the chapter never excuses him even while showing the spiritual roots of his decline.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on two Hebrew words used of the heart’s inclination. Jonathan’s ahav, love, in verses 1 and 3 is covenant loyalty in action, sealed by a berit, covenant, that costs him his own royal robe and weapons. Saul’s response, by contrast, is described from verse 9 as a qana, jealous, eye kept on David, a word that can mean holy zeal but here names a corrosive envy that will not let David alone. The same chapter that shows one man giving away his claim shows another man unable to let go of his.

typology.type: Jonathan stripping off his own royal robe for David
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true heir, who did not grasp equality with God but emptied Himself for the sake of others, Philippians 2:5-8.

typology.type: David, anointed yet hunted, beloved by the people yet opposed by the throne
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, anointed by the Spirit and beloved of the Father, yet rejected by the rulers of His day before His enthronement, John 1:11 and Acts 2:36.

typology.type: The LORD’s presence guaranteeing success despite hostility
typology.fulfillment: Christ’s assured vindication and exaltation by the Father despite the opposition of every earthly power, Acts 2:32-36.

study.christ: David in this chapter is marked out again and again as the man the LORD is with, prospering in everything he does even while the reigning king turns against him out of jealousy. He is already anointed, chapter 16, yet not yet enthroned, loved by the ordinary people even as the establishment schemes against him. This pattern, of God’s chosen king possessing the Father’s favor while facing the world’s hostility before receiving his throne, is fulfilled in Jesus, the greater Son of David, who was beloved of the Father, opposed by the rulers of His day, and exalted to His throne not by seizing it but by receiving it after faithful suffering, Philippians 2:8-11. Jonathan’s covenant of self-giving love, surrendering his own claim to the crown, is a small but real foreshadowing of the self-emptying love of the true King who gave up everything for those He loves.
study.god: God is the hidden engine of this whole chapter. Every one of David’s successes is explicitly traced to the LORD being with him, and Saul’s fear is explicitly traced to the LORD having departed from him, verse 12. God sovereignly overrules Saul’s repeated schemes, whether by spear or by a deadly bride price meant to use the Philistines as an assassin, showing that no human hostility, however well positioned, can thwart the LORD’s purpose for His anointed. God is also the giver of covenant love itself, since Jonathan’s loyalty mirrors the covenant faithfulness, hesed, that is God’s own character.

crossReferences.note: The Spirit of the LORD comes upon David and departs from Saul, the theological hinge that this chapter’s events flow from.
The command to love the LORD with covenant loyalty, the same ahav word used of Jonathan’s bond with David.
Wrath is fierce and anger is a flood, but who can stand before jealousy, a proverb Saul’s downward spiral illustrates exactly.
Christ’s self-emptying love, the pattern Jonathan’s gift of his robe and armor faintly foreshadows.
Jonathan and David’s covenant is renewed and tested further, showing the loyalty begun here was no passing moment.

study.oneStory: David the shepherd, already anointed in secret, now steps into public life bound in covenant love to Jonathan even as the reigning king turns against him in jealousy. The LORD’s unmistakable presence with David through every trial anchors the long story of the house of David, a story that runs forward to the greater Son of David, Jesus, loved by the Father, opposed by the rulers of this world, and at last given the throne that can never be shaken.

quiz.q: What did Jonathan do to seal his covenant with David?

options.text: He gave David his own robe, tunic, sword, bow, and belt.
options.note: Verse 4 records this costly, symbolic transfer of the prince’s own royal and military gear.

options.text: He simply promised David his friendship in words.
options.note: There were words of love, verses 1 and 3, but the covenant was sealed with a concrete, costly act, not words alone.

options.text: He asked Saul’s permission before befriending David.
options.note: No such request is recorded; the covenant is Jonathan’s own initiative toward David.

options.text: He challenged David to a contest of arms.
options.note: The opposite happened; Jonathan surrendered his own arms to David rather than contesting him.

quiz.q: What sparked Saul’s jealousy of David in this chapter?

options.text: The women’s song crediting David with tens of thousands and Saul with only thousands.
options.note: Verses 7-9 show this exact song is what turned Saul’s heart toward envy.

options.text: David’s marriage to Michal.
options.note: The marriage comes later and deepens Saul’s fear, but the jealousy began earlier, with the victory song.

options.text: Jonathan’s covenant with David.
options.note: The covenant is not presented as the spark for Saul’s jealousy; the song is.

options.text: David’s request for a larger command.
options.note: David made no such request; Saul himself gave David command, and jealousy arose from the song, not any request.

quiz.q: According to the chapter, why was Saul afraid of David?

options.text: Because the LORD was with David but had departed from Saul.
options.note: Verse 12 states this explicitly as the reason for Saul’s fear.

options.text: Because David commanded more soldiers than Saul.
options.note: David’s command grew, but the text names the LORD’s presence, not troop numbers, as the reason for Saul’s fear.

options.text: Because David refused to obey Saul’s orders.
options.note: David consistently obeyed and succeeded in what Saul sent him to do; disobedience is not the issue.

options.text: Because the Philistines had turned against Saul.
options.note: The Philistines are not described as opposing Saul directly here; his fear is of David, rooted in the LORD’s presence.

quiz.q: Why did the bride price of a hundred Philistine foreskins actually serve Saul’s scheme?

options.text: Saul intended for David to die at the hands of the Philistines while fulfilling the dowry.
options.note: Verse 25 states plainly that Saul intended to cause David’s death by the Philistines this way.

options.text: Saul wanted to test David’s courage before the wedding.
options.note: There was a real test of valor involved, but the text is explicit that Saul’s underlying motive was David’s death, not simply proving courage.

options.text: Saul wanted to strengthen ties with the Philistines.
options.note: The demand targeted Philistines as enemies to be struck down, not allies to befriend.

options.text: Saul had no ulterior motive in the request.
options.note: The text explicitly names Saul’s murderous intent behind the request.

quiz.q: What is the ongoing refrain that interprets David’s success throughout the chapter?

options.text: That the LORD was with David.
options.note: This phrase recurs at verses 12, 14, and 28, framing every success as God’s doing, not merely David’s skill.

options.text: That David was more skilled in battle than anyone else.
options.note: David’s skill is evident, but the text repeatedly credits the LORD’s presence as the deeper cause.

options.text: That the people always sided with whoever won the last battle.
options.note: The people’s love for David is noted, but it is presented as a response to God’s blessing on him, not mere crowd loyalty.

options.text: That Saul secretly favored David the whole time.
options.note: The opposite is true; Saul grew increasingly hostile even as David’s success continued.

quiz.q: How does Jonathan’s covenant with David point toward Christ?

options.text: The rightful heir freely yields his own claim and gives himself for the one God has chosen, foreshadowing Christ’s self-emptying love.
options.note: This mirrors Philippians 2:5-8, where Christ, the true heir, empties Himself for others rather than grasping His own glory.

options.text: It shows that royal succession never really mattered in Israel.
options.note: Succession mattered a great deal, which is exactly what makes Jonathan’s surrender of his claim so striking and costly.

options.text: It proves that Jonathan doubted David’s calling.
options.note: The covenant and the gift of his robe show confident recognition of David’s place, not doubt.

options.text: It has no meaningful connection to the gospel.
options.note: The self-giving pattern of a rightful heir yielding his claim genuinely foreshadows Christ’s own self-emptying love.

questions: Jonathan gave up his own claim to the throne out of love for David and trust in God’s choice. What claims or privileges is God asking you to release for the sake of someone else?
Saul’s jealousy grew from a single song, yet it hardened into a lifelong hostility. Where do you notice small comparisons or resentments starting to take root in your own heart?
The chapter repeats that the LORD was with David through danger, not around it. How does it change your view of hard seasons to know God’s presence does not mean the absence of opposition?
David kept serving faithfully even while Saul plotted against him. What would it look like for you to keep doing the next faithful thing while trusting God with an unresolved threat or conflict?
Jonathan’s covenant love cost him something real, his own robe and claim. Who in your life might need that kind of costly, self-giving loyalty from you this week?

translationNotes.note: The souls of Jonathan and David were knit together renders vivid Hebrew soul language for deep, bonded affection; it describes the closest kind of loyal friendship, not merely warm feeling.

Kept a jealous eye renders a Hebrew idiom of watching with suspicion and envy; the same root elsewhere describes zeal for the LORD, showing how the same intensity of heart can turn toward good or corrosive ends.

A spirit of distress renders a phrase the BSB footnote also gives as a harmful spirit; whichever nuance, the text is clear this affliction came upon Saul only after the LORD’s Spirit had departed from him, chapter 16:14.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Samuel 18:1-30. No em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file; hyphens in compound words such as son-in-law are retained as ordinary hyphens.
